text.title: Decision Framework and Service Decomposition
text.summary: When to choose microservices vs modular monolith, prerequisites, and decomposition strategies: business capability, DDD subdomains, volatility-based, and the Strangler Fig migration pattern.

section.title: Microservices Decision Framework

rule.statement: Do not adopt microservices until domain boundaries are well-understood and DevOps maturity (CI/CD, container orchestration, distributed tracing) is already in place.
rule.rationale: Premature decomposition without clear boundaries produces a distributed monolith: services that must be deployed together and share databases. This combines all the operational complexity of microservices with none of the autonomy benefits. Domain clarity and DevOps tooling are prerequisites, not afterthoughts.

list.item: Team < 10 developers: use monolith or modular monolith — operational overhead outweighs team autonomy benefit.
MVP / unclear domain: use modular monolith — extract services once boundaries are proven by usage.
Team 30+ developers with sub-team ownership, clear DDD boundaries, mature CI/CD, K8s, and observability: microservices justified.
Decision tree: domain well-understood? → No: modular monolith. Yes → 30+ developers? → No: modular monolith. Yes → mature DevOps? → No: build DevOps first. Yes → microservices.

section.p: Prerequisites before adopting microservices: strong DevOps culture, per-service CI/CD pipelines, container orchestration (Kubernetes), distributed tracing, clear DDD or business capability boundaries, team autonomy with Conway's Law alignment.

rule.statement: Decompose by business capability first; apply DDD subdomain classification (core / supporting / generic) to decide whether to build, buy, or use commodity services for each capability.
rule.rationale: Business capability alignment ensures each service maps to what the business does, not to technical layers. DDD classification prevents spending engineering effort on generic capabilities (authentication, payment) that commodity providers handle better.

list.item: By business capability: align service to a business function (Catalog, Inventory, Order, Payment, Shipping, User, Notification). Each service owns its data and releases independently.

By DDD subdomain — Core: build in-house, competitive advantage (e.g., recommendation engine). Supporting: build or buy, necessary but not differentiating (e.g., user auth). Generic: use existing commodity (e.g., payment via Stripe).

By volatility: separate stable domains (authentication, user profile) from frequently changing ones (pricing, promotions, search ranking). Stable services can be low-complexity; volatile ones need fast release cycles.

Strangler Fig: extract incrementally from monolith. Phase 1: monolith handles all traffic. Phase 2: new service handles a subset, monolith handles the rest. Phase 3: gradually shift traffic. Phase 4: retire monolith components.

rule.statement: Use the Strangler Fig pattern for migrating an existing monolith; never rewrite the entire monolith to microservices in a single release.
rule.rationale: Big-bang rewrites fail at a high rate. The Strangler Fig pattern allows incremental extraction, validation in production, and rollback at each phase. Each extracted service can be validated independently before the next extraction begins.
